source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
A grain depot uses an electric motor with a rated voltage of U = 380 \mathrm{V} and an internal resistance of R = 0.25 \Omega to transport grain. The counterweight and the cable connecting the motor to the trolley are parallel to the inclined plane. The trolley loaded with grain moves uniformly uphill along the inclined plane at a speed of v = 2 \mathrm{m/s}, with a current of I = 40 \mathrm{A}. After the motor is turned off, the trolley continues to move uphill a distance L, reaching the unloading point, at which time its velocity becomes zero. After unloading the grain, giving the trolley an initial downward velocity allows it to move uniformly downhill along the inclined plane. Given the mass of the trolley m_1 = 100 \mathrm{kg}, the mass of the grain m_2 = 1200 \mathrm{kg}, the mass of the counterweight m_0 = 40 \mathrm{kg}, and the acceleration due to gravity g = 10 \mathrm{m/s^2}, the frictional force experienced by the trolley during movement is proportional to the total weight of the trolley and the grain, with a proportionality constant of k. The counterweight remains off the ground throughout the process, and the mechanical friction loss of the motor itself and the mass of the cable are negligible.

1. Determine the value of the proportionality constant k.
2. Request the value of the uphill distance L.

image_dependency: requires_image=true and text_dominant=false indicate upstream marked the item as image-reliant
asset_registry_record shows one valid image exists and registry_passed=true
the problem describes an inclined-plane mechanical setup where a diagram can materially help identify directions and object relations
however, the question_text already contains most numerical values and setup details, so image dependence is high but not maximal
multi_step: the task has two requested outputs: first solve for k, then solve for uphill distance L
solving requires combining electrical power/motor relations with mechanics on an inclined plane
the text includes multiple states of motion: loaded uphill uniform motion, coasting after power-off, and unloaded downhill uniform motion
the answer is not directly extractive; it requires setting up and linking multiple equations across conditions
verifiability: answer_type is short_text with explicit target values for k and L
the outputs are quantitative and can be checked by substituting into standard physics equations
question_text provides the needed parameters numerically, enabling deterministic verification
asset registry indicates question and answer are present and registry_passed=true

risk_flags: image role is not explicitly described in the text; some needed reasoning may already be recoverable from text alone

decision_reasons: question, answer, and image assets are all present, and the registry passed with no recorded issues
initial scoring is strong: image_dependency_score=0.82, multi_step_score=0.91, and verifiability_score=0.95
the problem appears to be a recoverable and useful physics item with quantitative answers that can be deterministically checked
the only noted risk is limited ambiguity about how essential the image is, which is not sufficient to lower priority given the otherwise complete assets

The counterweight and the cable connecting the motor to the trolley are parallel to the inclined plane. The trolley loaded with grain moves uniformly uphill along the inclined plane at a speed of v = 2 \mathrm{m/s}, with a current of I = 40 \mathrm{A}. After the motor is turned off, the trolley continues to move uphill a distance L, reaching the unloading point, at which time its velocity becomes zero. After unloading the grain, giving the trolley an initial downward velocity allows it to move uniformly downhill along the inclined plane. Given the mass of the trolley m_1 = 100 \mathrm{kg}, the mass of the grain m_2 = 1200 \mathrm{kg}, the mass of the counterweight m_0 = 40 \mathrm{kg}, and the acceleration due to gravity g = 10 \mathrm{m/s^2}, the frictional force experienced by the trolley during movement is proportional to the total weight of the trolley and the grain, with a proportionality constant of k. The counterweight remains off the ground throughout the process, and the mechanical friction loss of the motor itself and the mass of the cable are negligible.

1. Determine the value of the proportionality constant k.
2. Request the value of the uphill distance L.
